falsification_rule_text: Not supported if the supply-elasticity-x-time-trend coefficient on net-of-housing real wage growth is statistically insignificant at the 5 percent level on cluster-robust SEs, or if its sign is opposite to predicted (supply-restricted metros showing more favourable net-of-housing wage growth than elastic metros). The hypothesis also requires that the coefficient be quantitatively meaningful: supply-restricted metros (bottom tercile elasticity) should show at least 0.5 percentage points per annum lower net- of-housing real wage growth than elastic metros (top tercile) over the sample period. Separately, if headline real wage growth and net-of-housing real wage growth are highly correlated across metros (correlation > 0.9), the framework's claim that headline series systematically mis-represent welfare is empirically weak.
